text: His prior incision can be seen from the latissimus flap, highlighting the incision with the blue drape around the area that was prepped and draped and the white dotted line where the prior incision existed.
The head is to the left, the feet are to the right.
Our surgery begins
with a recreation of the incision in the old prior scar.

Ideally, we would perform a posterolateral muscle sparing thoracotomy for this approach or a minimally invasive approach, but because he had undergone prior treatment, especially because this was a re-operation, we wanted to make sure that we went back through the old incision so that he did not have trouble.
Upon entry into the chest, we performed a meticulous dissection, including harvest of an intercostal nerve
muscle flap.
The intercostal muscle flap will allow us to buttress across the bronchus because we knew that it was possible that we would have to do a pulmonary arterioplasty or a pulmonary bronchoplasty.
This meticulous harvesting of the intercostal muscle flap involves making an incision along the inferior edge of the rib in which you plan to harvest the artery, nerve, and vein.
We then use a periosteal rib elevator
to scrape the periosteum down below the edge of the rib.
We then use the periosteum rib elevator to gently lift the artery, nerve, and vein off of that region.
Once we enter into the chest, you can see that there are severe adhesions throughout the chest wall.
We then begin to take those adhesions down.
The head is to the top of the field and you can see the superior vena cava and the vagus nerve just posterior to that with also
trachea behind that.
You can also see that we're dissecting the phrenic nerve away from the dense adhesions where the lung is densely adherent to the superior vena cava.
The patient had had prior radiation in this region and that caused the tissue to be densely adherent.
We then used a stapler only on the lung, not on the phrenic nerve area, to remove the lung off of the region
without having a significant air leak.
The operative field can be seen in the top left corner of the video.
By continuing to dissect around the right inferior pulmonary vein, we use a hand as well as a sucker tip to find that division point between the superior and inferior pulmonary veins.
The heart is to the right, the head is to the top of the field, and the feet are to the bottom.
The left index finger is being placed in between the superior and inferior pulmonary veins, and an umbilical tape is being placed between the superior and inferior pulmonary veins.
This umbilical tape will serve as a potential tourniquet around the right inferior pulmonary vein to obtain the first step in getting distal control in the event of pulmonary artery bleeding.
This umbilical tape can be snared onto a tourniquet, and the tourniquet can be used
to tighten up with a snap.

We're removing a lymph node in this region, and now we will begin to dissect the right superior pulmonary vein, as well as the adhesions to the superior vena cava, as well as the right pulmonary artery.
Obtaining proximal and distal control during cases like this for a potential pulmonary arterioplasty is an essential
technique or maneuver that will enable surgeons to gain control during difficult dissection.

Whenever a difficult dissection is anticipated, it's always important to try to take adhesions away, avoid working yourself into a hole to make sure that a meticulous attention to hemostasis is applied, and that the broad dissection allows adequate visualization of all essential structures.
In this view, the head is to the top of the field
The azagus vein is just above the lung hilum and the hand is now being placed around the right superior pulmonary vein structure.
By using a gentle manipulation with a sucker and a peanut dissector, you can see that we are dissecting all of the dense adhesions around this region.
We have taken the right branch.
We are now getting
a clamp around the right superior pulmonary vein.
The Harken clamp, made by Scanlon, is a nice instrument for coming around large hilar blood vessels.
The Harken clamp is gently passed around the superior pulmonary vein and another umbilical tape is passed around that right superior pulmonary vein.
Attached to that is another tourniquet that can be tightened up as a snare in the event of bleeding.
By obtaining control of the superior and inferior pulmonary veins, we have achieved distal control.
Now we will begin to obtain right superior pulmonary artery or right main pulmonary artery proximal control.
The truncus anterior, the posterior ascending pulmonary artery branches are all distal to this branch point.
If exposure in this area is difficult, intrapericardial control of the right
sided pulmonary artery can be obtained.
As you can see, the meningioma is just to the left of the pulmonary artery branch point.
We place another umbilical tape around the right pulmonary artery to pass it and then attach a tourniquet or a snare.
Obtaining proximal control of the pulmonary artery is an essential first step.
We then use a stapler to divide between the right upper lobe and the right middle lobe, going down towards the hilum.

By dividing that branch point between the right upper lobe and the right middle lobe, you can see the pulmonary vein branches going to the right upper lobe and the right middle lobe, sparing those vein branches going to the right middle lobe.
The right posterior pulmonary vein can be seen just deep to the staple line here, and a hook cautery is being used to elevate that vein and separate it from other pulmonary artery branches.
We then continue to take the truncus anterior branch with an endoscopic stapler, as you can see.
The venous branches and the truncus anterior branches are essential first steps of doing a right upper lobe lobectomy.

By taking the right superior pulmonary vein branch and the truncus anterior of the pulmonary artery, you begin the first step of performing a VATS right upper lobectomy or an open right upper lobectomy.
We then begin to cut the bronchus to elevate the menostatic meningioma
away from what it appears to be densely adherent to one of the bronchial branches.
By dissecting this, we obtain better control and achieve an R0 negative margin.
We then use the stapler to continue the posterior fissure and then use a TA stapler to staple across the bronchus and achieve a seal.
A lower profile stapler such as the TA stapler can help you in certain situations where you do not have enough room to perform an endogia stapler.
And now we begin to complete the lymphadenectomy, taking station 11R and 12R lymph nodes adjacent to the metastatic meningioma.
Once this is complete, we now want to cover the stump of the right upper lobe with an intercostal muscle flap.
We begin by placing sutures around that stump, and now we're identifying the right azagus vein as it courses over the hilum.
Individual sutures have been placed around the bronchial stump and the intercostal muscle is now being secured to that stump as a buttress.
Once the stump is secured and the sutures are cut, we will re-insufflate the lung, place chest tubes, and close.
This video was brought to you to show you that control of the pulmonary artery, proximal and distal, should be an essential part whenever you encounter a difficult situation.
